coredata.dc:title: Transparent nanofiber textiles with intercalated ZnO@graphene QD LEDs for wearable electronics
coredata.dc:creator: Lee, Kyu Seung
Shim, Jaeho
Park, Mira
Kim, Hak Yong
Son, Dong Ick
coredata.dc:description: Transparent human hair-based textiles, which are founded on a natural resource, have been fabricated by an electrospinning technique. Transparent human hair-based textiles are very useful for transparent wearable electronics because they have unique optical properties in the visible light region such as transparency of over ∼85%. In order to apply wearable electronic devices with transparent textiles, we fabricated sandwich structures with transparent flexible light emitting diodes (LEDs) embedded in transparent nanofiber textile utilizing ZnO@graphene quantum dots (ZGQDs) and demonstrated the fabrication process and characterization of ZGQD LEDs with textiles. We believe that these results will inspire new methods in wearable electronics and raise the hope of achieving even higher efficiencies by means of development and control of transparent textile ordering.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrospinning
Nanofibers
LEDs
ZnO@graphene QD
Wearable electronics

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the transparent textiles fabrication process. (a) Fabrication of Keratin + PVA solution. (b) Electrospinning process. (c) Fabrication of transparent textile after dipping in water.

SEM images of (a) before and (b) after dipping textiles. (c) Optical transmittance of electrospun hair/PVA textiles and transparent textiles. (d) Photographs of electrospun hair/PVA textiles before after dipping in water on the cloth. Schematic illustration of light transmission through the textiles (e) before and (f) after dipping in water.
(a) The schematic illustration of ZGQDs synthesis mechanism (b) TEM image of ZGQDs. (c) Raman spectra of ZGQDs. (d) Photoluminescence spectra of ZGQDs.
(a)–(e) the fabrication process of transparent nanofiber textiles with intercalated ZnO@graphene QD LEDs.

(a) Electroluminescence spectra of textile/ZGQDs LED/textile structure devices. (b) Luminance and current efficiency characteristics for ZGQDs LEDs with textile. (c) The external quantum efficiency and the luminous efficiency of ZGQDs LEDs with textile. (d) Energy band diagram of ZGQDs LEDs. (e), (f) Photograph of the blue light emission from textile/LED/textile structure devices and after bending (radius of curvature: R = 1 cm). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

head.ce:title: Transparent nanofiber textiles with intercalated ZnO@graphene QD LEDs for wearable electronics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Advanced Composite Materials, Korea Institute of Science and Technology, 92 Chudong-ro, Bongdong-eup, Wanju-gun, Jeonbuk, 55324, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Korea Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Organic Materials & Fiber Engineering, Chonbuk National University, 567 baekje-daero, Deokjin-gu, Jeonju, Jeonbuk, 54896, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Organic Materials & Fiber Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Transparent human hair-based textiles, which are founded on a natural resource, have been fabricated by an electrospinning technique. Transparent human hair-based textiles are very useful for transparent wearable electronics because they have unique optical properties in the visible light region such as transparency of over ∼85%. In order to apply wearable electronic devices with transparent textiles, we fabricated sandwich structures with transparent flexible light emitting diodes (LEDs) embedded in transparent nanofiber textile utilizing ZnO@graphene quantum dots (ZGQDs) and demonstrated the fabrication process and characterization of ZGQD LEDs with textiles. We believe that these results will inspire new methods in wearable electronics and raise the hope of achieving even higher efficiencies by means of development and control of transparent textile ordering.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, fiber-based structures are taking center stage in the field of wearable electronics. Fiber-based structures are not limited to the fabrication of garments, and have been actively applied by adding special weaving technology and composite materials. In addition, fiber structures are relatively stable, light and flexible when washed or stressed. Based on these advantages, fiber based electronics have been actively investigated for the electronic components such as transistors, antennas, connectors, and sensors, and energy conversion and health care [1–5]. Electrospinning has emerged as a useful technique and has been used to focus on the fabrication of ceramic, polymeric, and hybrid textiles with specific mechanical properties. Therefore, electrospun textiles are widely used for many applications such as fiber composites, drug delivery systems and engineering [6–9]. Transparent materials which have good mechanical properties are important in wearable optoelectronic display devices [10–13]. Also, electronic displays including quantum dot light emitting diodes (QDLEDs) for integration with clothing, textiles and nanopaper are being significantly developed in the field of eco-friendly wearable electronics [14]. Conventional QDs contain the cadmium (Cd) which classified as a hazardous substance according to a RoHS (Restriction on Hazardous Substances) directive. In order to surmount the prohibitions on Cd and other RoHS materials, ZnO@graphene quantum dots (ZGQDs) are used as an emitting layer in the QDLEDs structures.

In this paper, we present the easy and versatile fabrication of highly transparent electrospun human hair-based keratin/Poly (vinyl alcohol) blended NFs (hair/PVA NFs) by simple water treatment. In our previous work, we also investigated the optical and structural properties of hair-based NFs by various techniques [15]. Based on this study of environment-friendly textiles, we have fabricated transparent flexible ZGQDs LEDs with sandwich structures embedded in the textile in order to make wearable devices and investigated the fabrication process and characterization.

Urea, sodium disulfite (Na2S2O5), sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), acetone (CH3COCH3), ethanol (C2H5OH), glyoxal solution (40 wt% in H2O), PVA (Mw = 85,000–124,000 g/mol, 87–89% hydrolyzed) (polyethylenimine ethoxylated, 35-40 wt% in H2O average Mw ∼70,000) PEIE, N,N-Dimethyl formamide(HCON(CH3)2), anhydrous Chlorobenzene(C6H5Cl), methanol(CH3OH), 2-methoxyethanol(CH3OCH2CH2OH), Zinc acetate dehydrate ([Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O]), and graphite and poly (4-vinylphenol, Mw ∼25,000) PVP were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, Co. (St. Louis, USA). (PEDOT:PSS; VP AI 4083) was purchased from Clevios, Poly (4-butylphenyldiphenyl-amine) Poly-TPD was purchased from American Dye Source, (2,2,2 -(1,3,5-benzinetriyl)-tris (1-phenyl-1-H-benzimidazole)); TPBi was purchased from Luminescence Technology Corp.

In order to fabricate electrospin-blended NFs, a high-voltage power supply (Har-100*12, Matsusada Co., Tokyo, Japan) was used as the source of the electric field. The applied voltage and the distance between the capillary tip and the collector were fixed at 15 kV and 15 cm, respectively. As-spun randomly oriented hair/PVA blend NFs were collected on Teflon paper and dried under vacuum at 50 °C for 24 h and then cured at 120 °C for 10 min. In order to obtain a transparent textile, as-spun hair/PVA textile was immersed in water for 10 min and oven dried at 50 °C until completely dried.

Graphite oxide (GO) was well-dispersed in N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF, HCON(CH3)2), by sonication process for 30 min. Zinc acetate dihydrate (Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O) was dissolved in 200 ml of DMF, and then GO solution was added to the solution of zinc acetate dihydrate. The mixed solution was heated to 120 °C over a period of 5 h. The color of the ZGQDs then changed to greyish. Finally, the ZGQD solution was washed 4 times with ethanol and DI-water during centrifugation. Purified ZGQD solution was obtained after drying.

ZGQD LEDs were fabricated using a simple spin coating method. The QDLED devices were fabricated on a Polyethylene terephthalate (PET)/Indium Tin Oxide (ITO) anode with a sheet resistance of ∼100 Ωsq−1. The PET/ITO substrate was cleaned with a chemical solvent and then subjected to surface treatment with ultraviolet ozone for 10 min. The poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene): poly(styrene sulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS) solution was spin-coated and annealed at 110 °C for 30 min under ambient conditions. The poly(N,N′-bis(4-butylphenyl)-N,N′-bis(phenyl)-benzidine) (poly-TPD) polymer in chlorobenzene (1.5 wt%) solution was spin-coated and annealed at 110 °C for 1 h in N2 condition. Polyethylenimine ethoxylated (PEIE) was dissolved in 2-Methoxyethanol (0.15 wt%) as a buffer layer and was stirred for 12 h at room temperature; then, material was spin-coated and annealed at 110 °C for 10 min. The ZGQD powder in ethanol solution (5 mg/ml) was sonicated for 30 min and rapidly spin-coated; 2,2′,2″-(1,3,5-benzinetriyl)-tris(1-phenyl-1-H-benzimidazole) (TPBi) in methanol (0.5 wt%) was spin-coated and annealed at 50 °C for 10 min. After that, a 1 nm LiF and 100 nm Al cathode were deposited using a thermal evaporator. The textiles were attached to the both sides of the LEDs structures using epoxy and UV-curing agent method. Finally, the consolidated textile/ZGQDs LEDs/textile structures fabricated. The schematic illustration of the hair/PVA transparent textile fabrication method follows in Fig. 1 . The fabrication method of the transparent textiles was reported in a previous paper [16].

The surface morphologies were determined by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, S-7400 and Hitachi, Japan). The optical properties were investigated via spectroscopic analysis for hair/PVA transparent textiles (HP 8453 UV–visible spectroscopy system, Germany). The ZGQDs were characterized through transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Raman spectra, and photoluminescence (PL) spectra analyses. TEM images were obtained using a JEOL JEM-3010 TEM operating at 200 kV. Raman spectra for the ZGQDs were obtained using a Horiba Jobin Yvon-Labram HR UV–visible–NIR Raman microscope spectrometer with a 514 nm argon laser. The photoluminescence spectra were obtained using a Horiba NanoLog-C. The J–V characteristics of the fabricated QDLED embedded textile were measured by a programmable electrometer with built-in current and voltage measurement units (model 236, Keithley). Electroluminescence (EL) measurements were performed using a Spectra Scan PR-730.

Fig. 2 (a) and (b) presents FE-SEM images of the hair/PVA textiles. The average diameter of the fiber in the textiles was approximately 180 nm before dipping the textiles in water. On the other hand, the average diameter of the fiber in the textiles was approximately 210 nm after dipping in water. Most of the hair/PVA textiles are soluble in water. Therefore, we used Glyoxal as a cross linker in order to render these materials insoluble in water. It was observed that the hair/PVA textiles before dipping in water (Fig. 2(a), case 1) exhibited a randomly oriented bead-free orientation and had more interstices than the textiles after dipping in water (Fig. 2(b), case 2). In addition, the fibers in case 2 were slightly expanded and the fiber diameters are slightly broader than those in case 1, which could contribute to the optical transparency in the textiles. After fibers are dipped in water, smooth surfaces can be obtained in expanded textiles and these smooth surfaces can reduce the interstices of the internal textile and reduce the scattering of visible light by smoothing the surface of the hair/PVA textiles [12,13,16–18]. The hair/PVA textiles before dipping in water were not transparent, as shown in Fig. 2(c). However, the hair/PVA textiles exhibit high optical transparency (∼85% at λ = 550 nm) after dipping in water. Fig. 2(e) and (f) provides a schematic illustration of the light transmission through the nanofibers before and after dipping in water. The low transmittance of the textiles before dipping in water may be derived from the high surface roughness caused by large interstices between the textile fibers; surface roughness of the as-electrospun textiles made the surface more uneven and increased scattering of light. After dipping in water, the hair/PVA textiles changed into densely packed textiles and interstices between the textiles were smaller. It is means that the decrease of the surface roughness reduces the light scattering [15]. This surface-smoothing approach plays a role not only in imparting high optical transparency but also in acting as a moisture barrier for the hair/PVA textiles. As mentioned above, we produced a light-emitting device based on an environment-friendly synthesized ZGQDs. In addition, optical and structural characterization of the ZGQDs was carried out as follows. First, Fig. 3 (a) presents the synthesis mechanism of the ZGQDs from graphite oxide and zinc oxide QD. ZnO QDs were preferentially formed in the early stages. Then, three kinds of chemical reactions occurred at the outermost edge of the ZnO QDs and zinc-oxygen-carbon bonding was locally created [20]. The synthesis process of the ZGQDs has been reported in our previous study [20]. Fig. 3 (b) provides a TEM image of the ZGQDs, which are the ZnO QDs covered by graphene. The magnified image of the area in the red box, including the ZGQDs and black lines, shows the area of graphene. Raman spectra of the ZGQDs are shown in Fig. 3(c). In the Raman spectra of the ZGQDs, the peaks of related with the ZnO QD were observed for the ZnO-E2 high mode (433 cm−1) and ZnO 1LO (477 cm−1), and then the peaks of related with the graphene were observed D band (1346 cm−1), G− band (1571 cm−1), G+ band (1594 cm−1) and 2D band (2682 cm−1). Fig. 3(d) presents the PL spectra of the ZGQDs. The PL main peak for the ZnO QDs is observed at wavelengths of 393 nm (3.15 eV), which coincide well with the known values of excitonic emission with a bandgap at room temperature. The two additional emissions peaking at 404 nm (3.06 eV) and 418 nm (2.96 eV) can be seen to related with new energy level by carbon (C)-oxygen (O) bonding in the ZnO QDs [19,20]. The new energy level by carbon (C)-oxygen (O) bonding from reference is indicated, which shows at the energy levels near the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) level using a simple model with density functional theory (DFT) [19,20].

Fig. 4 shows the fabrication process of ZGQD LED devices with QD monolayer using electrostatic bonding. The QDLED device consists of the patterned ITO as anode electrode, the PEDOT:PSS as a hole injection layer (HIL), the poly-TPD as a hole transport layer (HTL), the PEIE as an adhesion and interface control layer for the ZGQDs monolayer on PEIE film, the ZGQDs as the emission layer (EML), the TPBi as the hole injection layer (HIL) and LiF/Al (cathode), as shown in Fig. 4(a). The QDLED devices were fabricated using ZGQDs as the emission layer. In order to form a uniform ZGQD monolayer, the PEIE was coated on the poly-TPD surface by spin coating process because the PEIE layer includes negatively charged functional groups (-OH) or positively charged functional groups (-NH), as shown in inset of Fig. 4(a) [19]. The PEIE layer with positively charged functional groups can bond to the ZGQDs surfaces with functional groups epoxy, hydroxyl and carboxyl) by electrostatic interaction between PEIE and ZGQDs. As a result, we achieved a uniform ZGQDs monolayer.

Fig. 4(b)-(e) shows the fabrication process of the textile/ZGQDs LED/textile devices. The epoxy was coated on the PET surface, which is the opposite side of the LED structure and the textile was attached to the PET surface. Also, the epoxy was coated on the cathode electrode (Al) of the LEDs from the opposite side, and then a UV-curing agent was used on the cathode electrode (Al) of the LEDs. Finally, the textile was attached to the cathode electrode (Al) of the LEDs.

Fig. 5 shows the optical characteristic analysis results for the fabricated ZGQDs LEDs embedded in textile. Fig. 5(a) shows the EL at the applied voltages. Two distinctive emissions appear at around 425 nm (2.91 eV) and 455 nm (2.72 eV) [19]. Fig. 5(b) shows the luminance–voltage characteristics of the textile/ZGQDs LED/textile devices. At an applied bias of 8 V, the device produced a maximum luminance of 70.19 cd m−2. The maximum values of external quantum efficiency, current efficiency and power efficiency of the device are 0.03%, 0.02 cd A−1 and 0.01 l m W−1, respectively (Fig. 5(c)). In the energy band diagrams of the ZGQDs LEDs (Fig. 5(d)), the electrons and holes can be seen to be coming from the electrode with forward bias and through the hopping-mechanism due to recombination in the light-emitting layer. In this case, the electrons can pass through the LiF/Al and TPBi (ETL). Holes can pass through the PEDOT:PSS (HIL) to the Poly-TPD (HTL), to the PEIE and to the ZGQDs. Also, the accumulated electrons are recombined with the holes in the ZGQDs interface [21] and blue light is generated. The potential barriers to hole injection from the ITO to the HOMO level of the PEDOT:PSS, to the HOMO level of the poly-TPD, and to the valence band of the ZnO QDs are defined as 0.2, 0.4 and 2.1 eV, respectively [20,22,23]. The holes under applied forward bias tunnel into the HOMO level of the PEDOT:PSS, to the poly-TPD, and then to the valence band (VB) of ZnO. The carriers are transported through the hopping mechanism. The TPBi with the lowest work function (2.8 eV) is used as an electron transport layer (ETL), as well as a hole blocking layer (HBL); this is accomplished using the chemical method deposition rather than the widely used thermal evaporated LiF/Al method [24,25]. Fig. 5(e) provides an image of the light emission of textile/ZGQDs LEDs/textile devices at the applied bias before bending and after bending. We hold onto both side edges of the textile device to bend it (radius of curvature is 1 cm); the device can then emit light (Fig. 5(f)). For this reason, sandwich structures with ZGQD LEDs embedded in textile are extremely applicable to wearable devices.

In summary, transparent textiles were successfully fabricated by electrospinning method. We have demonstrated a simple method for the production of transparent textiles and have analyzed the structural and optical properties. We fabricated transparent nanofiber textiles with intercalated ZGQDs LED devices in order to protect QLED devices against the degradation. The luminescence of the ZGQDs LED devices embedded in the textile was 70.19 cd/m2. We observed the light emission of the textile/ZGQDs LEDs/textile devices at the applied bias before bending and after bending. These results indicate that sandwich structures with QDLEDs embedded in transparent textiles are a desirable application for the wearable electronics.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author, Dong Ick Son, would like to show his appreciation for the financial support of the KIST Institution Program. Also, this work was supported under the framework of the international cooperation program managed by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF-2015K2A1B8047602). And, this research was supported by Nano Material Technology Development Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning (2016M3A7B4900135).
